hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Pre-eductor attack hose: 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to foam eductor
Post-eductor attack hose: 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose from foam eductor to foam nozzle
nozzles: Foam nozzle matched to eductor flow
appliances: In-line foam eductor rated at 95 GPM
Foam eductor required inlet pressure: 200 PSI
Yellow foam concentrate bucket with lid and pickup tube
sceneElements.elevation: Level roadside operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: The foam line is operating through a 95 GPM eductor, and a backup water line is flowing 185 GPM. What is the total flow requirement for the vehicle-fire operation?
problems.instructorExplanation: The foam eductor controls the foam-line flow, but total incident flow also includes the backup line.
problems.explainMistake: Do not ignore the backup line when the question asks for total operation flow.

problems.title: Foam Eductor Inlet Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is set to 215 PSI for the foam eductor discharge. The hose between the engine and eductor loses 15 PSI. What pressure reaches the foam eductor inlet?
formulaBreakdown: Eductor inlet pressure = engine discharge pressure - pre-eductor friction loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: This checks the eductor logic from the opposite direction: pump pressure minus the hose loss before the eductor equals eductor inlet pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add post-eductor nozzle pressure here. The question is asking what reaches the eductor inlet.

problems.title: Pre-Eductor Friction Loss
problems.question: Engine 181 pumps through 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose to the foam eductor. The eductor flow is 95 GPM. Using C = 15.5, how much friction loss is present before the eductor?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Pre-eductor hose uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the hose between Engine 181 and the foam eductor is included for this friction loss question. At 95 GPM through 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose, the friction loss rounds to 15 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the hose after the eductor when calculating pre-eductor friction loss. The question asks only for the hose section before the foam eductor.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is pumping an in-line foam eductor operation. The hose from the engine to the eductor is 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose flowing 95 GPM, with 15 PSI friction loss. The eductor requires 200 PSI at its inlet. Do not add the post-eductor nozzle pressure to the engine discharge calculation. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: Foam operation PP = pre-eductor FL + required eductor inlet pressure + elevation.
Required foam eductor inlet pressure = 200 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must overcome friction loss before the foam eductor and still deliver the required inlet pressure to the eductor. The required pump discharge pressure is 215 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not set the pump at only 200 PSI. The eductor needs 200 PSI at its inlet, so the pump must also overcome the friction loss between the engine and the eductor.

problems.title: Foam Concentrate Needed
problems.question: The foam operation flows 95 GPM using 3% foam concentrate for 10 minutes. How many gallons of foam concentrate are needed for the assigned operating period?
problems.instructorExplanation: Foam concentrate use is based on total foam solution flow and the selected foam percentage. At 95 GPM for 10 minutes using 3% foam, the operation requires 28.5 gallons of concentrate.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the water supply hose size or pump pressure to calculate concentrate use. Foam concentrate is calculated from flow, time, and foam percentage.
